title: Older adults and immunocompromised people
summary: People who are older or immunocompromised have a lower margin for dehydration, pneumonia and medication side effects. Seek advice earlier rather than relying on symptom severity alone.
evidenceSnapshot: Early testing and prescription antiviral assessment can matter for influenza or COVID-19. Medication review is essential because sedatives, anticholinergics, NSAIDs and decongestants can cause disproportionate harm.

interventions.name: Early clinical contact for flu-like illness
interventions.finding: High-risk people may benefit from early testing, monitoring and antiviral treatment.
interventions.practical: Contact a GP or urgent-care service promptly, particularly within the first 48 hours of influenza symptoms.
interventions.mechanism: Earlier treatment can limit viral replication and complications.
interventions.cautions: Do not wait for severe fever; older adults may have muted fever responses.

interventions.name: Hydration and intake monitoring
interventions.finding: Prevents or identifies dehydration, which can worsen confusion, kidney function and falls.
interventions.practical: Offer regular fluids if not fluid-restricted; track intake, urine and alertness.
interventions.cautions: People with heart or kidney failure need an individual fluid plan.

interventions.name: Medication reconciliation
interventions.finding: Reduces duplicate ingredients, interactions and adverse effects.
interventions.practical: Use one written list and ask a pharmacist to review cold-and-flu choices.
interventions.cautions: Extra caution with anticoagulants, sedatives, blood-pressure medicines, kidney disease and glaucoma.

interventions.name: Vaccination and household prevention
interventions.finding: Influenza vaccination and practical infection-control measures reduce risk of severe disease and transmission.
interventions.practical: Annual influenza vaccination and current recommended COVID vaccination; improve ventilation and avoid close contact when ill.
interventions.mechanism: Primes immune responses and reduces exposure.
interventions.cautions: Immune response may be weaker but vaccination still provides important protection.

practicalSteps: Record baseline cognition, mobility, temperature, breathing and fluid intake so changes are noticeable.
Arrange someone to check in if the person lives alone.
Keep a pulse oximeter only if a clinician has explained how to interpret it; symptoms still matter.
Have an up-to-date medicine and allergy list ready for telehealth or urgent care.
avoidOrCare: Avoid sedating antihistamines unless specifically advised; they can cause confusion, urinary retention and falls.
Avoid NSAIDs without advice in kidney disease, dehydration, ulcers, anticoagulant use or heart failure.
Avoid decongestants with uncontrolled hypertension, arrhythmia or urinary retention.
Do not assume absence of fever means absence of serious infection.

myths.claim: It is safe to watch and wait until an older person develops a high fever.
myths.reality: Serious infection may present with weakness, confusion, falls or reduced intake without a dramatic fever.
Common cold medicines can interact with regular medicines and worsen cognition, blood pressure, kidneys or bladder function.

redFlags: New confusion, collapse, marked weakness or inability to manage usual activities
Breathlessness, chest pain, blue lips, oxygen level below the person's clinician-set threshold or rapid deterioration
Very low intake, little urine, dizziness or inability to keep medicines down
Fever or respiratory symptoms after transplant, chemotherapy, high-dose steroids or another major immune-suppressing treatment
unsettled: Exact thresholds for antiviral treatment depend on the cause, immune condition, local circulation and current Australian guidance; individual assessment is required.
